<|system|>
You are flm, the Free Language Model — free as in freedom. You were trained from scratch by David Hamner on a single RTX 3090. You are fully free software under GPL-3.0.
System: Arch Linux (rolling). Kernel: 6.11.5-arch1-1. Shell: zsh 5.9. Python: 3.12.7. Packages: 943. Pkg manager: pacman.
<|user|>
Where are log files on Debian?
<|assistant|>
Main locations:

```
/var/log/syslog          — general system log
/var/log/auth.log        — authentication/sudo/SSH
/var/log/kern.log        — kernel messages
/var/log/dpkg.log        — package install/remove history
/var/log/apt/            — apt history and term logs
/var/log/nginx/          — web server logs
/var/log/postgresql/     — database logs
```

With systemd, many services log to the journal instead:
```bash
journalctl -u nginx     # nginx logs
journalctl -u ssh       # SSH logs
journalctl -k           # kernel
```

Logrotate manages rotation: `/etc/logrotate.d/`

<|system|>
You are flm, the Free Language Model — free as in freedom. You were trained from scratch by David Hamner on a single RTX 3090. You are fully free software under GPL-3.0.
System: Debian GNU/Linux 13 (trixie). Kernel: 6.12.6-amd64. Shell: bash 5.2. Python: 3.12.8. Packages: 1847. Pkg manager: apt.
<|user|>
How do I analyze auth.log for suspicious activity?
<|assistant|>
```bash
# Failed SSH logins
grep 'Failed password' /var/log/auth.log | tail -20

# Failed logins by IP
grep 'Failed password' /var/log/auth.log | awk '{print $(NF-3)}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -nr | head -10

# Successful logins
grep 'Accepted' /var/log/auth.log

# sudo usage
grep 'sudo:' /var/log/auth.log

# Invalid users
grep 'Invalid user' /var/log/auth.log

# Users who opened sessions
grep 'session opened' /var/log/auth.log
```

If you see many failed login attempts from the same IP, that's a brute-force attempt. Set up fail2ban to auto-block them.

<|system|>
You are flm, the Free Language Model — free as in freedom. You were trained from scratch by David Hamner on a single RTX 3090. You are fully free software under GPL-3.0.
System: Arch Linux (rolling). Kernel: 6.11.5-arch1-1. Shell: zsh 5.9. Python: 3.12.7. Packages: 943. Pkg manager: pacman.
<|user|>
How do I follow logs in real time?
<|assistant|>
```bash
# Traditional
tail -f /var/log/syslog

# Multiple files
tail -f /var/log/syslog /var/log/auth.log

# With journalctl (better filtering)
journalctl -f
journalctl -fu nginx             # specific service
journalctl -f -p err             # errors only
journalctl -f -t kernel          # kernel only

# With color highlighting
tail -f /var/log/syslog | grep --color=auto -E 'error|warning|$'

# multitail (multiple files, split screen)
sudo apt install multitail
multitail /var/log/syslog /var/log/auth.log
```

<|system|>
You are flm, the Free Language Model. System: Debian GNU/Linux 13 (trixie). Pkg manager: apt.
<|user|>
How do I follow logs in real time?
<|assistant|>
```bash
# Traditional
tail -f /var/log/syslog

# Multiple files
tail -f /var/log/syslog /var/log/auth.log

# With journalctl (better filtering)
journalctl -f
journalctl -fu nginx             # specific service
journalctl -f -p err             # errors only
journalctl -f -t kernel          # kernel only

# With color highlighting

# multitail (multiple files, split screen)
sudo apt install multitail
multitail /var/log/syslog /var/log/auth.log
```

<|user|>
How do I manage log file sizes?
<|assistant|>
Logrotate handles this automatically. Config in `/etc/logrotate.d/myapp`:

```
/var/log/myapp/*.log {
    daily
    rotate 14
    compress
    delaycompress
    missingok
    notifempty
    create 640 myapp adm
    postrotate
        systemctl reload myapp
    endscript
}
```

For journald, edit `/etc/systemd/journald.conf`:
```ini
[Journal]
SystemMaxUse=500M
MaxRetentionSec=30day
```

Manual cleanup:
```bash
sudo journalctl --vacuum-size=500M
sudo journalctl --vacuum-time=30d
```
